dubois-souls-passing-of-the-first-born.alt: W. E. B. Du Bois seated with his wife Nina and their young daughter Yolande in a formal family portrait, circa 1901
dubois-souls-passing-of-the-first-born.caption: W. E. B. Du Bois with his wife Nina and daughter Yolande, photographed around 1901, the family whose firstborn son Burghardt's death Du Bois mourns in this chapter.
dubois-souls-passing-of-the-first-born.repository: New York Public Library Digital Collections

dubois-souls-golden-fleece.alt: African American laborers operating a cotton gin and pressing baled cotton at Dahomey, Mississippi, 1899
dubois-souls-golden-fleece.caption: A Mississippi cotton gin at Dahomey in 1899, the machinery and labor at the heart of the cotton economy Du Bois traces through the crop-lien debt that bound the tenant farmer.
dubois-souls-golden-fleece.repository: Library of Congress, Prints and Photographs Division (digital ID ppmsca.53149)

dubois-souls-golden-fleece.alt: Men gathered on the porch of a rural country store with gasoline pumps on a dirt road, Gordonton, North Carolina, 1939
dubois-souls-golden-fleece.caption: A Southern country store in Gordonton, North Carolina, the kind of plantation-and-crossroads store where the crop-lien system ran tenant farmers into the perpetual debt Du Bois describes.
dubois-souls-golden-fleece.repository: Library of Congress, Prints and Photographs Division (FSA/OWI Collection, digital ID fsa.8b33922)

dubois-souls-sons-of-master-and-man.alt: An African American man climbing an exterior stairway marked as the colored entrance to a movie theater, Belzoni, Mississippi, 1939
dubois-souls-sons-of-master-and-man.caption: A man entering the segregated colored entrance of a movie house in Belzoni, Mississippi, a documentary record of the rigid color line whose relations of Black and white Du Bois dissects in this chapter.
dubois-souls-sons-of-master-and-man.repository: Library of Congress, Prints and Photographs Division (FSA/OWI Collection)

dubois-souls-sorrow-songs.alt: Studio group portrait of the Fisk University Jubilee Singers, circa 1870s
dubois-souls-sorrow-songs.caption: The Fisk Jubilee Singers of Nashville, whose concert tours carried the spirituals to the world, the very sorrow songs Du Bois exalts as America's most original art in this closing chapter.
dubois-souls-sorrow-songs.photographer: Published by the American Missionary Association
dubois-souls-sorrow-songs.repository: Library of Congress, Prints and Photographs Division (LCCN 2010647805)

dubois-souls-training-of-black-men.alt: Four African American women students seated on the steps of a building at Atlanta University, Georgia, 1899 or 1900
dubois-souls-training-of-black-men.caption: Four students on the steps at Atlanta University around 1900, from W. E. B. Du Bois's own album of African American life, embodying the college-trained leadership he champions in this chapter.
dubois-souls-training-of-black-men.repository: Library of Congress, Prints and Photographs Division (Daniel Murray Collection, assembled by W. E. B. Du Bois for the 1900 Paris Exposition)
dubois-souls-training-of-black-men.license: Public domain (no known restrictions on publication)
